diagrams.title: Basic Self-Referential Structure Template
diagrams.description: Visual representation of a self-referential structure definition in C, showing both the code syntax and its memory layout with data and pointer fields
elements: Rectangle representing struct node
Two internal fields: 'data' (integer) shown as a box on the left, 'next' (struct node*) shown as an arrow pointing right
Arrow labeled 'next' pointing outside the struct to indicate self-reference
Memory layout below showing consecutive memory blocks: data field (4 bytes), next pointer (8 bytes on 64-bit)
Labels: 'struct node' at top, 'int data' label, 'struct node *next' label
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the fundamental structure of a self-referential structure, understanding both the syntax and how it's represented in memory with a data field and a pointer to the same structure type

diagrams.title: Chain of Three Connected Nodes
diagrams.description: Three self-referential structures linked together in a sequence, demonstrating how nodes connect through their next pointers
elements: Three rectangular nodes arranged horizontally: Node 1, Node 2, Node 3
Each node split into two sections: 'data' containing values (10, 20, 30) and 'next' containing a pointer
Arrow from Node1.next pointing to Node2
Arrow from Node2.next pointing to Node3
Node3.next shown as NULL (empty) with a grounded symbol
Labels showing memory addresses: 0x1000, 0x1050, 0x10A0
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how multiple self-referential structures are linked together, understanding the concept of a linked chain and NULL termination

diagrams.title: Memory Layout: Heap Allocation for Linked Nodes
diagrams.description: Shows how self-referential structures are allocated in heap memory dynamically, with pointers stored in the stack pointing to heap nodes
elements: Stack area on left showing 'struct node *head' pointer at address 0x0001
Heap area on right showing three allocated node blocks at addresses 0x2000, 0x2050, 0x20A0
Arrows from stack pointer 'head' to first node at 0x2000
Each heap node shows internal 'next' pointer arrows connecting to next node
Free space shown in heap below allocated nodes
Dotted box around entire heap area labeled 'Dynamic Memory (malloc)'
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the difference between stack (pointers) and heap (allocated nodes) and how dynamic memory allocation enables flexible data structure creation

diagrams.title: Static Array vs Self-Referential Structure Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of static array and self-referential linked structure in memory
elements: Left side: Contiguous memory blocks labeled 'int arr[5]' with consecutive addresses 0x0000-0x0010, all cells filled
Right side: Dispersed memory blocks labeled 'Linked List' scattered in heap at different addresses, connected by pointer arrows
Array shown with fixed size box, Linked list shown with growing arrows
Annotations: Array has 'Fixed Size' and 'Contiguous Memory' labels, Linked list has 'Dynamic Size' and 'Non-contiguous Memory' labels
Arrow showing array cannot grow, curved arrow showing linked list can extend
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the key differences between static arrays and dynamic linked structures, learning about memory contiguity, fixed vs variable size, and insertion/deletion efficiency

charts.title: Static Allocation vs Linked (Dynamic) Allocation Comparison Table
charts.content: Table with columns: Feature, Static Allocation (Array), Linked Allocation. Rows: Memory Size (Fixed at compile time vs Dynamic at runtime), Memory Contiguity (Contiguous blocks vs Scattered in heap), Insertion/Deletion (O(n) shifting required vs O(1) pointer adjustment), Access Method (Random access via index vs Sequential access via pointers), Memory Waste (Possible unused space vs No waste, exact allocation), Implementation Complexity (Simple vs More complex)

charts.title: Process of Creating and Linking Self-Referential Nodes
charts.content: Flowchart steps: Start → Declare struct with self-pointer → Allocate memory using malloc() → Check if allocation successful (if NULL, handle error) → Initialize data field → Set next pointer to NULL (for first node) or existing node address → Return pointer to node → End. Decision diamond at allocation check, rectangular process boxes for each step

charts.title: Data Structures Built Using Self-Referential Structures
charts.content: Classification hierarchy: Self-Referential Structures → Linear: Singly Linked List, Doubly Linked List, Circular Linked List → Non-Linear: Binary Trees, Binary Search Trees, AVL Trees, Graphs (adjacency list), General Trees → Applications: Stack (using linked list), Queue (using linked list), File System, Browser History, Music Playlist

branches: Definition: Structure containing pointer to same type
Key Components: Data field + Self pointer (next/prev)
Memory: Heap allocation via malloc(), Non-contiguous
Operations: Create node, Insert node, Delete node, Traverse
Data Structures: Linked Lists (Singly/Doubly/Circular), Trees (Binary/BST), Graphs
Advantages: Dynamic size, Efficient insert/delete, No memory wastage
Disadvantages: No random access, Extra memory for pointers, Sequential traversal
Real-world: Music playlists, Browser history, File systems, Social network graphs
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting self-referential structures to their applications, advantages, disadvantages, and the data structures they enable
